Technology
Following Conventions
Begins in grade 2.
Utilize book handing skills.
This standard does not apply to literature at this grade level.

canonical_name: Kindergarten Language Arts: Foundational Skills — Book Handling and Conventions
coherence_reason: The set mixes actual skills (book handling, conventions), broad topics (interpersonal communication, technology), and grade progression notes that do not describe skills.

subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: With help, your child can use new words they learn from talking, being read to, or reading together.
Your child can write regularly over short and long periods for different tasks and audiences, including time to research and revise.
With help, your child can pick an event and use drawing, talking, or writing to share about it.
With help, your child can find pictures or objects that match what is in a familiar story.
Your child can print many uppercase and lowercase letters.
With help, your child can join in reading and talking about nonfiction books.
Your child can say the main sounds that letters make, especially consonants.
With help, your child can join in group activities to learn about a topic and help write about it.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Language Arts: Foundational Skills — Reading, Writing, and Speaking
coherence_reason: The standards span multiple foundational skills, including vocabulary, writing, phonics, reading comprehension, and speaking/listening, which are related but could be considered distinct sub-concepts.
split_topics: Foundational Reading Skills (phonics, letter recognition, comprehension)
Writing and Speaking Skills (writing, drawing, sharing, group activities)

subject: Language Arts
sample_standards: Your child understands why Europeans explored and settled in Alabama and how this affected trade, health, and land.
Your child earned credit in an elective class that encourages civic participation.
Your child can tell the difference between goods (things you buy) and services (help people provide).
Your child can compare how different countries and regions trade with each other.
Your child understands how population changes relate to geography, economy, and history in Alabama and the world.
Your child can tell the difference between needs (things you must have) and wants (things you would like) for families, schools, and communities.
Career Research
Your child understands that fresh water in rivers, lakes, underground, and glaciers is limited and can last longer by recycling and using less.
canonical_name: Kindergarten Social Studies: Economics — Goods, Services, Needs, and Wants
coherence_reason: Standards address economics, exploration, trade, civic participation, geography, population, career research, and environmental stewardship, which are distinct topics and not coherently grouped.
split_topics: Economics: Goods, Services, Needs, and Wants
